No Poverty.reason: The course covers "Economic and Fiscal Issues" and "policy formation," which are key to creating pro-poor development strategies and policy frameworks as per Target 1.b.

Quality Education.reason: As a graduate seminar, this course provides quality tertiary education (Target 4.3) and develops skills in policy analysis and leadership, relevant for sustainable development (Target 4.7).

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course explicitly covers "Economic and Fiscal Issues," directly relating to policies for sustained economic growth and productivity as outlined in Targets 8.1 and 8.2.

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: The focus on "Land Planning Issues" directly addresses infrastructure development (Target 9.1), a key component of this goal.

Reduced Inequalities.reason: The study of "Public Governance" and "Economic and Fiscal Issues" relates to adopting fiscal and social policies to achieve greater equality, as per Target 10.4.

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: The course has a dedicated series on "Land Planning Issues," which is central to sustainable urbanization and human settlement planning (Target 11.3).

Climate Action.reason: The topics of "Public Governance" and "Land Planning" could potentially include integrating climate change measures into national policies (Target 13.2), but this is not explicitly stated.

Life on Land.reason: The theme "Land Planning Issues" is relevant to integrating ecosystem and biodiversity values into national and local planning, as per Target 15.9.

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: The course is fundamentally about "Public Governance," policy formation, and leadership, directly aiming to build effective and accountable institutions and decision-making processes (Targets 16.6, 16.7).

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The course structure, which invites "practitioners from industry, government, and academia," exemplifies the multi-stakeholder partnership approach promoted in Target 17.17.
